title: Electronic Transaction Act (Draft Framework — Bangladesh)
title_en: Electronic Transaction Act — Draft Provisions for Bangladesh
description: Bangladesh has been developing a comprehensive Electronic Transaction Act to modernize the legal framework for digital commerce beyond what the ICT Act 2006 covers. The draft framework establishes principles for electronic contracts, digital payments, consumer protection in e-commerce, and cross-border digital trade. The framework builds on the ICT Act's provisions for electronic records and digital signatures while adding specific provisions for modern digital business models.

provisions.title: Section 1. Purpose and Scope
provisions.content: The purpose of the Electronic Transaction Act is to facilitate the conduct of transactions and the exercise of rights and the performance of obligations in electronic form. The Act applies to electronic transactions conducted between persons resident or doing business in Bangladesh, and to electronic transactions that have effects within Bangladesh. The Act establishes the legal equivalence of electronic records and communications with their paper equivalents, recognizes the legal validity of electronic contracts and digital payments, establishes consumer protections for e-commerce, and provides a framework for digital identity and authentication. The Act complements and supplements the provisions of the Information and Communication Technology Act 2006 and the Digital Security Act 2018. In the event of any inconsistency between this Act and those Acts, the provisions of this Act shall prevail in relation to electronic transactions.

provisions.title: Section 4. Formation of Electronic Contracts
provisions.content: A contract shall not be denied legal effect, validity or enforceability solely because it is formed by electronic means. An offer and an acceptance of an offer may be expressed by electronic records. Unless agreed otherwise, an offer expressed in an electronic record shall be deemed to have been received when it is accessible by the addressee at an electronic address designated by the addressee, or if no address is designated, when the addressee becomes aware of the offer. An acceptance expressed by an electronic record shall be deemed to have been received when it is accessible to the offeror. An electronic contract is concluded when the acceptance is received by the offeror. In consumer transactions conducted through automated systems, the trader shall provide the consumer with an opportunity to review the order and to correct errors before the final confirmation of the order. Failure to provide this opportunity may result in the consumer having the right to withdraw from the transaction.

provisions.title: Section 7. Consumer Protection in E-Commerce
provisions.content: Persons conducting e-commerce activities in or affecting Bangladesh shall comply with the following consumer protection requirements: (a) clear, accurate and complete disclosure of the identity of the trader, including name, contact address and registration details; (b) accurate description of goods and services offered, including price, applicable taxes and delivery charges; (c) clear statement of the payment terms, delivery arrangements and applicable return and refund policies; (d) secure processing of consumer payment information; (e) right of withdrawal within fourteen days of receipt of goods without penalty and without requirement to provide reasons; (f) provision of a confirmed receipt for all electronic orders. Traders shall not use deceptive or unfair practices in e-commerce, including the use of dark patterns that manipulate consumer decision-making. The Directorate of National Consumer Rights Protection shall have jurisdiction to receive and investigate consumer complaints relating to e-commerce.

provisions.title: Section 10. Digital Payment Systems
provisions.content: The Bangladesh Bank shall have authority to license, regulate and supervise digital payment service providers in Bangladesh, including mobile financial services, payment service providers, and digital wallet operators. Licensed payment service providers shall: implement appropriate security measures to protect payment transactions and consumer data; maintain sufficient capital and liquidity to meet their payment obligations; comply with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing requirements; provide consumers with receipts for all transactions; and maintain records of all transactions in accordance with Bangladesh Bank requirements. Consumers shall be protected against unauthorized transactions through a liability framework requiring payment service providers to bear the liability for unauthorized transactions unless the consumer has acted negligently or fraudulently. Payment service providers shall report suspicious transactions to the Bangladesh Financial Intelligence Unit.

provisions.title: Section 12. Cross-Border Electronic Commerce
provisions.content: Bangladesh shall promote the development of cross-border e-commerce through bilateral and multilateral agreements and through the adoption of internationally recognized standards for electronic transactions. Goods and services purchased through cross-border e-commerce shall be subject to the relevant customs, taxation, and import control requirements of Bangladesh. The National Board of Revenue shall establish a simplified customs clearance procedure for low-value e-commerce imports to facilitate the participation of Bangladeshi consumers in global e-commerce. Bangladeshi businesses conducting cross-border e-commerce shall comply with the laws of the countries to which they sell goods or provide services, particularly with regard to consumer protection and data privacy. The Government shall negotiate digital trade provisions in free trade and economic partnership agreements to reduce barriers to cross-border e-commerce.

provisions.title: Section 15. Online Dispute Resolution
provisions.content: The Government shall promote the development of online dispute resolution (ODR) mechanisms for the resolution of disputes arising from electronic transactions, particularly those involving low-value consumer transactions. ODR mechanisms shall be: accessible online without requirement for in-person attendance; affordable or free for consumers; fair, independent and impartial; operated by qualified dispute resolution practitioners; and binding where both parties have agreed to their binding nature. The Bangladesh Competition Commission shall maintain an online consumer dispute portal for the resolution of e-commerce disputes. The courts of Bangladesh shall recognize settlements reached through ODR mechanisms as having the legal effect of settlement agreements. Traders conducting e-commerce with consumers in Bangladesh shall inform consumers about available ODR mechanisms and shall co-operate with ODR proceedings initiated by consumers.

provisions.title: Section 18. Digital Identity Framework
provisions.content: The Government shall establish a National Digital Identity Framework to enable citizens and businesses to authenticate themselves securely in electronic transactions. The National Digital Identity System shall: provide every citizen with a unique digital identity based on the National Identity Card system maintained by the Election Commission; enable secure authentication for government services and, where agreed by service providers, for private sector services; protect the privacy of citizens by minimizing the disclosure of personal data in authentication transactions; be interoperable with digital identity systems of partner countries to facilitate cross-border digital commerce and government services. The use of the digital identity system for authentication in private sector transactions shall be voluntary for citizens. Service providers that use the digital identity system shall comply with the security and privacy requirements established by the Government.

provisions.title: Section 20. Platform Economy Regulation
provisions.content: Digital platforms that facilitate transactions between buyers and sellers in Bangladesh shall be subject to the following regulatory requirements: (a) registration with the competent authority; (b) maintenance of systems to prevent illegal goods and services from being listed; (c) implementation of mechanisms for consumers to report illegal or misleading listings; (d) provision of clear information to both buyers and sellers about their respective rights and obligations; (e) maintenance of escrow or other mechanisms to protect consumer payments; (f) provision of contact information for consumer dispute resolution. Digital platforms that provide gig economy services shall comply with labour laws applicable to the services facilitated through their platform. The Government may issue additional regulations for specific types of digital platforms including food delivery, transportation network companies, and digital financial services marketplaces.

provisions.title: Section 22. E-Government Services
provisions.content: All government services capable of digital delivery shall progressively be made available through electronic means. The a2i Programme (Aspire to Innovate) under the Prime Minister's Office shall coordinate the digitalization of government services. The National e-Service Portal (ekpay.gov.bd and services.govt.bd) shall serve as the primary digital gateway for citizen interactions with government. Government agencies shall: accept electronic filings, applications and payments; issue electronic certificates and licences with digital signatures; maintain electronic records of all transactions and communications; and provide digital receipts for all government transactions. Citizens shall not be required to physically visit government offices for services that can be provided electronically. The e-Government framework shall prioritize accessibility for persons with disabilities and for citizens in rural and remote areas.

definitions.definition: A transaction conducted, recorded or stored by electronic means, including contracts formed, executed or evidenced in electronic form, and electronic payments.
The right of a consumer who has entered into an e-commerce contract to withdraw from the contract within fourteen days of receipt of goods without penalty and without requirement to provide reasons.

A digital service that facilitates interactions between two or more distinct user groups, including e-commerce marketplaces, gig economy platforms, social media platforms, and digital financial services marketplaces.
Mechanisms for the resolution of disputes arising from electronic transactions conducted through online processes, without requiring in-person attendance of the parties.
